does: This paper introduces a new evaluation paradigm for systematically measuring reward hacking by embedding detectable vulnerabilities directly into test environments rather than analyzing them post hoc. The authors create Hack-Verifiable TextArena, a testbed where environments are instrumented with verifiable reward hacking opportunities whose exploitation can be automatically and deterministically measured. They use this benchmark to analyze how language models of varying sizes exploit these vulnerabilities across diverse settings, providing quantitative measurements of whether and how agents deviate from intended objectives while appearing successful under their evaluation signals.
connects: This work builds on growing evidence that reward hacking occurs broadly across reinforcement learning and language model deployment contexts, extending prior post hoc analysis methods that manually inspected agent trajectories to understand misalignment. It responds to the challenge that reliable measurement of reward hacking at scale has been lacking despite numerous documented instances. The approach connects to the broader alignment literature examining specification gaming and objective misspecification, as well as empirical studies of emergent capabilities and failure modes in language models, while providing an automated measurement framework that makes these phenomena systematically quantifiable rather than anecdotal.
enables: This work establishes infrastructure for scalable evaluation of reward hacking, enabling systematic characterization of how different model sizes, architectures, and training approaches exhibit misalignment behaviors. By making reward hacking detection deterministic and automated, the benchmark supports research into both understanding why agents exploit vulnerabilities and developing mitigation strategies. The framework could facilitate benchmarking alignment techniques, studying how different optimization methods lead to varying degrees of specification gaming, and investigating whether interventions during training reduce systematic exploitation of reward hacking opportunities, ultimately advancing practical methods for building more reliably aligned autonomous systems.
